does: This paper investigates why aligned large language models remain vulnerable to jailbreaks by introducing Refusal-Escape Directions (RED)—continuous perturbation directions that shift models from refusing to answering harmful requests while preserving semantic interpretation of the harm. Through a mechanistic lens, the authors prove that RED can be decomposed into operator-level contributions across the model architecture, identifying normalization layers, residual connections, and other components as key vulnerability sources. They demonstrate that jailbreaks represent behavior transitions along these geometric directions in activation space, providing a unified framework for understanding why safety measures fail and quantifying the fundamental trade-offs between model capability and alignment.
connects: This work builds on recent mechanistic interpretability studies that have identified latent features and representation shifts underlying jailbreak success, extending beyond discrete prompt-level analysis to continuous geometric perspectives. It connects to prior research on adversarial perturbations in neural networks, safety-utility trade-offs in aligned models, and activation-space analysis of model behavior. The paper responds to open questions about structural vulnerabilities in alignment approaches and relates to work studying how models represent harmful versus helpful content. By decomposing vulnerabilities into operator-level sources, it advances beyond black-box adversarial attack studies toward white-box understanding of alignment failures informed by recent interpretability successes.
enables: This framework enables more targeted safety improvements by identifying specific architectural components and operations that enable refusal escape, pointing toward interventions at layer and operator granularity rather than broad fine-tuning. The decomposition of RED into operator contributions facilitates the design of architecturally-informed alignment techniques and principled safety-utility trade-off analysis. The continuous perturbation view opens directions for measuring alignment robustness geometrically and understanding fundamental limits of safety training. Future work can leverage these structural insights to develop provably harder-to-jailbreak architectures, improve interpretability of safety mechanisms, and establish whether certain design choices inherently limit achievable alignment robustness.
